text.title: ops-subscription-models Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for ops-subscription-models. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Offer both monthly and annual billing. Present annual as "2 months free" (not "17% off"). For most SaaS, annual reduces churn by 3–5x compared to monthly-only.
rule.rationale: Annual billing creates a 12-month commitment window before the next churn decision. "2 months free" is tangible (users can visualize two months); "17% off" is abstract.

rule.statement: Use Good-Better-Best (max 4 tiers) as default. Free tier must be limited enough that a real user would want to upgrade within 30 days of hitting the limit.
rule.rationale: Too-generous free tiers eliminate upgrade motivation. The most common subscription failure pattern is a free tier that fully satisfies the core use case.

rule.statement: Track Net Revenue Retention (NRR) as the single health metric. NRR above 100% means expansion revenue exceeds churn; below 100% means the business is leaking faster than it's filling.
rule.rationale: NRR captures both churn and expansion in one number. A business with 8% monthly churn but 12% expansion can grow despite the churn. NRR is the metric investors use to assess subscription business quality.

rule.statement: Implement dunning automation before launch: retry failed payments on Day 1, 3, and 7 with accompanying emails; downgrade to free on Day 10. This recovers 20–40% of would-be churned revenue.
rule.rationale: 15–25% of subscription cancellations are involuntary (expired cards, insufficient funds). Without automated recovery, this becomes silent permanent churn. Most customers with failed payments want to keep the subscription.

rule.statement: Instrument lifecycle stage transitions and trigger interventions at each: onboarding email Day 1, check-in Day 3, conversion offer Day 7 (for trials); usage-drop alert for at-risk; save offer at cancellation; win-back at Day 30 post-churn.
rule.rationale: Each lifecycle stage has a different intervention ROI. Day 1 onboarding email has the highest open rate of any email the user will ever receive (50–70%). At-risk outreach is cheaper than win-back.
